revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:أراي هاكوسيكي]][[arz:اراى هاكوسيكى]][[bo:ཨ་རའི་ཧ་ཀུ་སེ་གི]][[ca:Arai Hakuseki]][[cs:Hakuseki Arai]][[de:Arai Hakuseki]][[en:Arai Hakuseki]][[es:Arai Hakuseki]][[eu:Arai Hakuseki]][[fa:آرای هاکوسکی]][[fr:Arai Hakuseki]][[gl:Arai Hakuseki]][[it:Arai Hakuseki]][[ja:新井白石]][[ko:아라이 하쿠세키]][[no:Arai Hakuseki]][[pl:Hakuseki Arai]][[pt:Arai Hakuseki]][[ru:Араи Хакусэки]][[sv:Arai Hakuseki]][[uk:Араї Хакусекі]][[zh-classical:新井白石]][[zh:新井白石]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Arai Hakuseki - Japanischer Gelehrter.jpg|birth_date={{birth date|1657|3|24}}|death_date={{death date|1725|6|29}}|birth_place={{ill|Edo|qid=Q215646|1=Edo}}|death_place={{ill|Sendagaya|qid=Q263347|1=Sendagaya}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|economist|qid=Q188094|1=Economist}}; {{ill|historian|qid=Q201788|1=Historian}}; {{ill|philosopher|qid=Q4964182|1=Philosopher}}; {{ill|poet|qid=Q49757|1=Poet}}; {{ill|politician|qid=Q82955|1=Politician}}; {{ill|writer|qid=Q36180|1=Writer}}|notable_works={{ill|Ezo-shi|qid=Q17218998|1=Ezo-shi}}; {{ill|Hankanfu|qid=Q11625079|1=Hankanfu}}; {{ill|Koshitsū|qid=Q11410979|1=Koshitsū}}; {{ill|Oritaku Shiba no Ki|qid=Q3007804|1=Oritaku Shiba no Ki}}; {{ill|Sairan Igen|qid=Q259708|1=Sairan Igen}}; {{ill|Seiyō Kibun|qid=Q3013516|1=Seiyō Kibun}}; {{ill|Tokushi Yoron|qid=Q3007480|1=Tokushi Yoron}}|religion={{ill|Confucianism|qid=Q9581|1=Confucianism}}|native_name=新井白石|resting_place={{ill|Kōtoku-ji|qid=Q97310751|1=Kōtoku-ji Temple}}}}
{{Short description|Japanese scholar and official (1657–1725)|qq=}} {{Use mdy dates|date=December 2014|qq=}} {{family name hatnote|Arai|lang=Japanese|qq=}} {{Infobox writer | name = Arai Hakuseki | image = Arai Hakuseki - Japanischer Gelehrter.jpg | imagesize = 200px | caption = Arai Hakuseki | pseudonym = | birth_date = March 24, 1657 | birth_place = Edo | death_date = June 29, 1725 | death_place = Edo | occupation = Neo-confucian scholar, academic, administrator, writer | subject = Japanese history, literature }} {{nihongo|'''Arai Hakuseki'''|新井 白石|extra=March 24, 1657 – June 29, 1725|qq=}}<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arai Hakuseki {{!|qq=}} Japanese statesman {{!|qq=}} Britannica|url=https://www.britannica.com/biography/Arai-Hakuseki|access-date=2021-12-11|website=www.britannica.com|language=en|qq=}}</ref> was a {{ill|Confucianist|en|Confucianist|qid=Q9397505|lt=Confucianist|1=Ru (Confucian scholar)}}, [[:en:scholar-bureaucrat|scholar-bureaucrat]], academic, administrator, writer and politician in Japan during the middleof the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, who advised the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|qid=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' {{ill|Tokugawa Ienobu|en|Tokugawa Ienobu|qid=Q366734|lt=Tokugawa Ienobu|1=Tokugawa Ienobu}}.<ref>Screech, Timon. (2006). ''Secret Memoirs of the Shoguns: Isaac Titsingh and Japan, 1779–1822'', pp.65–66.</ref><ref name=Sansom2>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334–1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |isbn=0804705259 |page=35|qq=}}</ref> His personal name was '''Kinmi''' or '''Kimiyoshi''' (君美). '''Hakuseki''' (白石) was his pen name. His father was a {{ill|Kururi Domain|en|Kururi Domain|qid=Q1002840|lt=Kururi|1=Kururi Domain}} [[:en:Han (country subdivision)|han]] samurai '''Arai Masazumi''' (新井 正済). ==Biography== Hakuseki was born in {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} and from a very early age displayed signs of {{ill|genius|en|genius|qid=Q185030|lt=genius|1=Genius}}. According to one story, at the age of three Hakuseki managed to copy a Confucian book written in {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|qid=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}}, character by character. Because he was born on the same year as the {{ill|Great Fire of Meireki|en|Great Fire of Meireki|qid=Q1336847|lt=Great Fire of Meireki|1=Great Fire of Meireki}} and because he was hot tempered and his brow would crease looking like 火 or "fire", he was affectionately called ''Hi no Ko'' (火の子) or ''child of fire''. He was a retainer of {{ill|Hotta Masatoshi|en|Hotta Masatoshi|qid=Q936047|lt=Hotta Masatoshi|1=Hotta Masatoshi}}, but after Masatoshi was assassinated by {{ill|Inaba Masayasu|en|Inaba Masayasu|qid=Q11596944|lt=Inaba Masayasu|1=Inaba Masayasu}}, the {{ill|Hotta clan|en|Hotta clan|qid=Q751004|lt=Hotta clan|1=Hotta clan}} was forced to move from {{ill|Sakura Domain|en|Sakura Domain|qid=Q910993|lt=Sakura|1=Sakura Domain}} to {{ill|Yamagata Domain|en|Yamagata Domain|qid=Q908769|lt=Yamagata|1=Yamagata Domain}} then to {{ill|Fukushima Domain|en|Fukushima Domain|qid=Q909294|lt=Fukushima|1=Fukushima Domain}} and the domain's income declined. Hakuseki offered to leave, becoming a ''{{ill|Rōnin|en|Rōnin|qid=Q212418|lt=Rōnin|1=Rōnin}}'' and studied under Confucianist [[Kinoshita Junan|Kinoshita Jun'an]]. He was offered a post by the largest ''han'', that of {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|qid=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain|1=Kaga Domain}}, but he offered the position to a fellow samurai. In 1693, Hakuseki was called up to serve by the side of {{ill|Manabe Akifusa|en|Manabe Akifusa|qid=Q6746560|lt=Manabe Akifusa|1=Manabe Akifusa}} as a "brain" for the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and shogun {{ill|Tokugawa Ienobu|en|Tokugawa Ienobu|qid=Q366734|lt=Tokugawa Ienobu|1=Tokugawa Ienobu}}. He went on to displace the official {{ill|Hayashi clan (Confucian scholars)|en|Hayashi clan (Confucian scholars)|qid=Q2975266|lt=Hayashi|1=Hayashi clan (Confucian scholars)}} advisers to become the leading confucianist for Ienobu and {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|en|Tokugawa Ietsugu|qid=Q366722|lt=Tokugawa Ietsugu|1=Tokugawa Ietsugu}}. While some of Hakuseki's policies were still carried out after Ienobu's death, after the 6th shogun, Tokugawa Ietsugu, died and {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|qid=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune|1=Tokugawa Yoshimune}}'s rule began, Hakuseki left his post to begin his career as a prolific writer of Japanese history and Occidental studies. He was buried in [[Asakusa]] (current day [[:en:Taitō, Tokyo|Taitō, Tokyo]]), Hoonji temple but was later moved to {{ill|Nakano, Tokyo|en|Nakano, Tokyo|qid=Q234087|lt=Nakano, Tokyo|1=Nakano, Tokyo}}, Kotokuji temple. ==Economic policy== [[File:Tokugawa coinage.jpg|thumb|An export ban of {{ill|Tokugawa coinage|en|Tokugawa coinage|qid=Q5976974|lt=Tokugawa coinage|1=Tokugawa coinage}} was imposed by Arai Hakuseki in 1715.<ref>[https://books.google.com/books?id=pezQo6jf6N4C&pg=PA15 ''Lever of empire'' Mark Metzler p.15]</ref>]] Under the top [[Rōjū]], [[Abe Seikyo]], with strong support from Ienobu, he launched [[Shōtoku no chi]], a series of economic policies designed to improve the shogunate's standing. By minting new and better quality currency, inflation was controlled. Calculating from trade records, Hakuseki deduced that fully 25% of gold and 75% of silver in Japan had been spent on trades with foreign countries.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Totman|first1=Conrad|title=Early Modern Japan|date=1993|publisher=University of California Press|location=Berkeley and Los Angeles|isbn=978-0-520-20356-3|page=[https://archive.org/details/earlymodernjapan0000totm/page/283 283]|url=https://archive.org/details/earlymodernjapan0000totm/page/283|qq=}}</ref> Concerned that Japan's national resources were at risk, he implemented a new trade policy, the ''Kaihaku Goshi Shinrei'' (海舶互市新例), to control payments to Chinese and Dutch merchants by demanding that instead of precious metals, products like silk, porcelain, and dried seafoods should be used for trading. However, the beneficial effects of this policy were limited as the trade of precious metals from [[:en:Tsushima Domain|Tsushima]] and {{ill|Satsuma Domain|en|Satsuma Domain|qid=Q676187|lt=Satsuma|1=Satsuma Domain}} was uncontrolled by the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|qid=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}''. He also simplified rituals for welcoming the {{ill|Joseon|en|Joseon|qid=Q28179|lt=Joseon|1=Joseon}} dynasty's ambassadors, in the face of opposition from the Tsushima Confucianist [[Amenomori Hōshu]].{{Citation needed|date=August 2023|qq=}} ==Constitutional policy== {{Unreferenced section|date=August 2023|qq=}} Hakuseki applied the [[:en:mandate of heaven|mandate of heaven]] to both the emperor and the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|qid=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}''. Since there had been no revolution to change Japan's basic institutions, he argued that the shogun was subordinate to the emperor and that in showing good governance, moral fortitude and respect to the emperor a shogun proved that he held {{ill|Divine right of kings|en|Divine right of kings|qid=Q839452|lt=divine right|1=Divine right of kings}}. He also traced Tokugawa family roots back to the [[Minamoto clan]] and thus to a line of imperial descent in order to show that Ieyasu's political supremacy had been fitting. To strengthen the shogun's power and maintain national prestige he proposed changing the title to koku-ō – nation-king. ==Selected works== Hakuseki's published writings encompass 237 works in 390 publications in 6 languages and 3,163 library holdings.<ref>[http://www.oclc.org/research/activities/identities/default.htm WorldCat Identities] {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101230150412/http://www.oclc.org/research/activities/identities/default.htm |date=December 30, 2010 |qq=}}: [http://www.worldcat.org/identities/lccn-n79-107683 Arai, Hakuseki 1657–1725]</ref> {{dynamic list|qq=}} * 1709 – 本朝軍器考 * 1709 – {{nihongo|''{{ill|Sairan Igen|en|Sairan Igen|qid=Q259708|lt=Sairan Igen|1=Sairan Igen}}''|采覧異言,|''Collected views and strange words''|qq=}}.<ref name="s66">Screech, p. 66.</ref> * 1711 – {{nihongo|''Hōka shiryaku''| |''Brief history of currency''|qq=}}, also known as ''Honchō hōka tsūyō jiryaku'' ("Short Account of the circulation of currency in this realm").<ref>Screech, p. 65.</ref> ** _________. (1828). ''Fookoua Siriak: traité sur l'origins des richesses au japon'' (translation of ''Hōka shiryaku'' by Julius Klaproth). Paris. {{in lang|fr|qq=}} (1712). ' * 1712 – {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tokushi Yoron|en|Tokushi Yoron|qid=Q3007480|lt=Tokushi Yoron|1=Tokushi Yoron}}''|読史余論,|''Lessons from History''|qq=}}.<ref name="s66"/> * 1715 – {{nihongo|''{{ill|Seiyō Kibun|en|Seiyō Kibun|qid=Q3013516|lt=Seiyō Kibun|1=Seiyō Kibun}}''|西洋記聞,|''Record of things heard from the West''|qq=}}.<ref name="s66"/> A work describing the Occident, based on Hakuseki's conversations with {{ill|Giovanni Battista Sidotti|en|Giovanni Battista Sidotti|qid=Q326973|lt=Giovanni Battista Sidotti|1=Giovanni Battista Sidotti}} * 1729 – 蝦夷志 * 1760 – 同文通考 * 1805 – 東雅 * 1894 – ''[[Hankanfu]]'' (藩翰譜). A list of daimyo's family tree * 1936 – 新井白石集 * 1964 – 戴恩記 * 1977 – 新井白石全集 * 1977 – 新編藩翰譜 * 1981 – 新令句解 * ''Koshitsu'' (古史通). A work that detailed ancient history of Japan * ''[[:en:Oritaku Shiba-no-ki|Oritaku Shiba-no-ki]]'' (折りたく柴の記). A diary and memoir ==Notes== {{Reflist|2|qq=}} ==References== *{{cite book | author=Arai Hakuseki Trans. [[Joyce Ackroyd]] | title=Told Round a Brushwood Fire: The Autobiography of Arai Hakuseki | publisher=Princeton University Press | year=1980 | isbn=0-691-04671-9|qq=}} *{{cite book | author=Arai Hakuseki Trans. Joyce Ackroyd | title=Lessons from History: The Tokushi Yoron | publisher=University of Queensland Press | year=1982 | isbn=0-7022-1485-X | url-access=registration | url=https://archive.org/details/lessonsfromhisto0000arai |qq=}} *{{cite journal | last=Ackroyd | first=Joyce | title=Correspondence | journal=Monumenta Nipponica | volume=40 | pages=97–106 | date=Spring 1985|qq=}} * Brownlee, John S. (1997) ''Japanese historians and the national myths, 1600–1945: The Age of the Gods and Emperor Jimmu.'' Vancouver: {{ill|University of British Columbia Press|en|University of British Columbia Press|qid=Q7895143|lt=University of British Columbia Press|1=University of British Columbia Press}}. {{ISBN|0-7748-0644-3|qq=}} Tokyo: {{ill|University of Tokyo Press|en|University of Tokyo Press|qid=Q842460|lt=University of Tokyo Press|1=University of Tokyo Press}}. {{ISBN|4-13-027031-1|qq=}} *Brownlee, John S. (1991). ''Political Thought in Japanese Historical Writing: From Kojiki (712) to Tokushi Yoron (1712).'' Waterloo, Ontario: Wilfrid Laurier University Press. {{ISBN|0-88920-997-9|qq=}} *{{cite journal|last=Daehwan |first=Noh |title=The Eclectic Development of Neo-Confucianism and Statecraft from the 18th to the 19th Century |journal=Korea Journal |date=Winter 2003 |url=http://www.ekoreajournal.net/upload/html/HTML43412.html |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20110614035130/http://www.ekoreajournal.net/upload/html/HTML43412.html |archivedate=June 14, 2011 |qq=}} *{{cite journal | last=Henderson | first=Dan Fenno | title=Chinese legal studies in early 18th century Japan | journal=Journal of Asian Studies | volume=30 | pages=21–56 | year=1970 | doi=10.2307/2942722 | jstor=2942722 | issue=1 | publisher=The Journal of Asian Studies, Vol. 30, No. 1| s2cid=159910239 |qq=}} *{{cite journal | last=Kazui | first=Tashiro | journal=Journal of Japanese Studies | title=Foreign Relations during the Edo Period: Sakoku Reexamined | volume=8 | date=Summer 1982 | pages=283–306 | doi=10.2307/132341 | jstor=132341 | author2=Videen, Susan Downing | issue=2 | publisher=Journal of Japanese Studies, Vol. 8, No. 2|qq=}} * {{ill|Timon Screech|en|Timon Screech|qid=Q2434995|lt=Screech|1=Timon Screech}}, Timon. (2006). ''Secret Memoirs of the Shoguns: Isaac Titsingh and Japan, 1779–1822.'' London: [[:en:RoutledgeCurzon|RoutledgeCurzon]]. {{ISBN|0-7007-1720-X|qq=}} {{UNESCO Collection|qq=}}
[[en:Arai Hakuseki]]
<!-- for more information see [[:Template:Infobox writer/doc]] --><!-- 1882 – 西洋紀聞 --> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ar:أراي هاكوسيكي]] [[arz:اراى هاكوسيكى]] [[bo:ཨ་རའི་ཧ་ཀུ་སེ་གི]] [[ca:Arai Hakuseki]] [[cs:Hakuseki Arai]] [[de:Arai Hakuseki]] [[es:Arai Hakuseki]] [[eu:Arai Hakuseki]] [[fa:آرای هاکوسکی]] [[fr:Arai Hakuseki]] [[gl:Arai Hakuseki]] [[it:Arai Hakuseki]] [[ja:新井白石]] [[ko:아라이 하쿠세키]] [[no:Arai Hakuseki]] [[pl:Hakuseki Arai]] [[pt:Arai Hakuseki]] [[ru:Араи Хакусэки]] [[sv:Arai Hakuseki]] [[uk:Араї Хакусекі]] [[zh:新井白石]] [[zh-classical:新井白石]]
